title: Discos and Dragons

jokes.text: DISCO SUCKS! shouted during the school disco promotional video/assembly
jokes.setup: Students are being subjected to a chipper disco promotion with upbeat music ('Foxy's Hot Number' playing), clearly aimed at getting them excited
jokes.punchline: A defiant 'DISCO SUCKS!' cuts through the enthusiasm mid-song — pure punk-rock rebellion against institutional cheerfulness
jokes.explanation: Economical and character-specific — Ken's anti-disco stance becomes a running thread. The interruption of cheerful institutional music with a blunt two-word rejection is well-timed and sets up Ken's arc perfectly. Not exceptional writing but functionally strong as an episode-opening character statement.

jokes.text: Joan Jett's 'Bad Reputation' plays as the title sequence — a girl in a military jacket singing defiantly while scenes of teen awkwardness play
jokes.setup: The show presents itself as being about outcasts with 'bad reputations'
jokes.punchline: The visual irony: the 'freaks' and 'geeks' shown are awkward, sweet, and profoundly non-threatening — nothing like the badass the song describes
jokes.explanation: The show's persistent ironic use of its own theme song — the gap between 'bad reputation' and the actual sweetness of these kids — is a structural comedy device. Recurring across the series so carries rewatch value. Not a discrete joke but a tonal comedic statement.

jokes.text: Sam says he'll only let girls sign his yearbook so his future kids will think he was a big stud
jokes.setup: The geeks are excited about getting their yearbooks signed, a normal teen rite of passage
jokes.punchline: Sam's plan to curate his yearbook for a hypothetical future audience is both sweetly delusional and self-aware of his current unpopularity
jokes.explanation: A nicely observed joke about teenage insecurity — the gap between Sam's social reality and his aspirational self-image is the joke. Character-specific and grounded. Not exceptional but genuinely funny in context.

jokes.text: Neal: 'They always write the same thing. Neal, you're a wild and crazy guy. Never a wild and sexy guy.'
jokes.setup: Neal is sharing the fantasy that yearbook signers will reveal secret crushes
jokes.punchline: His complaint is the precise distinction between 'wild and crazy' (what he gets) versus 'wild and sexy' (what he wants) — the gap perfectly encapsulates his self-image versus reality
jokes.explanation: One of the tighter jokes in the episode. The specificity of Neal's complaint — not that no one finds him sexy, but the exact wrong compliment he always receives — is economical and character-rich. 'Never a wild and SEXY guy' lands because it reveals both his awareness of his reputation and his futile desire to be perceived differently. Very Neal.

jokes.text: Jocks dump everything out of Neal's locker while he's standing right there — 'GEEK CLEAN-OUT!'
jokes.setup: Neal just complained about never being seen as sexy; he's in his own little world of yearbook fantasies
jokes.punchline: Reality intrudes immediately and literally — his locker contents dumped out the moment he's most vulnerable in his self-image
jokes.explanation: Functional setup for Neal's subsequent rant but not particularly original. The timing is good — immediately after his 'sexy guy' lament — but 'jocks bully geeks' is the most standard sitcom beat. Serves plot more than comedy.

jokes.text: Neal's rant: 'They think clean-outs are funny. They think wedgies are funny. They think swirlies are funny. Where do they find time to play sports?'
jokes.setup: Neal is furious about being cleaned out and cataloguing jock cruelty
jokes.punchline: The list escalates in expected ways then ends with an absurd pragmatic question — not moral outrage but a time-management observation
jokes.explanation: Strong joke structure — the list form builds expectation for righteous anger, then the punchline pivots to a completely different register (logistical concern). The final question is unexpected and characteristically Neal: analytical rather than emotional. Good economy, well-earned.

jokes.text: Sam asks 'What's so geeky about us?' — immediately followed by Harris appearing with a new D&D handbook
jokes.setup: Sam sincerely asks what makes them geeks while they're in the hallway feeling sorry for themselves
jokes.punchline: Harris immediately appears, triumphantly announcing a new Dungeons & Dragons handbook as 'good news' — answering Sam's question without knowing it
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural joke — Sam asks the question, Harris provides the comic answer without awareness. The timing of Harris's entrance, the enthusiasm with which he announces the D&D news as 'good news,' and the obliviousness all work together. Good structural comedy that doesn't need a punchline line because the situation IS the punchline. Strong economy.

jokes.text: Harris arrives holding new D&D handbook immediately after Neal asks 'What's so geeky about us?'
jokes.setup: Neal just asked what's geeky about them, building toward some dignity
jokes.punchline: Harris arrives literally brandishing a Dungeons & Dragons handbook announcing Friday night plans — answering the question completely
jokes.explanation: A quick structural callback that works as a second punchline to Neal's rhetorical question. The D&D handbook as visual answer is economical and character-true. Doesn't need dialogue to land — the image does the work.

jokes.text: Daniel casually reveals he's paying Dave Fleury to cheat: 'Uh, yeah, but, um, don't tell anybody.'
jokes.setup: Ken asks, apparently as a throwaway question, if Daniel is paying Dave to help him cheat
jokes.punchline: Daniel's completely casual, almost cheerful confirmation — and the pathetic request for secrecy — delivered with zero shame
jokes.explanation: Daniel's casual self-incrimination is very much in character — his complete lack of pretense or defensiveness about doing something clearly wrong is the joke. The 'don't tell anybody' is particularly good: half-hearted and obviously useless since he just told them.

jokes.text: Mr. Fleck smoking: 'Sorry about the smoke. These things are gonna kill me. I know I might look cool with a cigarette in my hand, but, remember, it's just a crutch.'
jokes.setup: The A.V. teacher/counselor figure intercepts the geeks, smoking casually
jokes.punchline: He delivers an anti-smoking message while actively smoking, positioning his own cigarette as both a cool prop and a crutch he's simultaneously endorsing and warning against
jokes.explanation: Good character work — Mr. Fleck is the well-meaning adult who undermines his own advice by example. The specificity of 'I know I might look cool with a cigarette' (a self-aware vanity admission) makes this more than generic hypocrisy comedy. He's not unaware; he just doesn't care.

jokes.text: Mr. Fleck's mime-acted graph of jock life arcs from 'Pee Wee Football!' through 'I just blew my knee out' to 'What? I'm fired? Hand me that bottle of booze, man.'
jokes.setup: Mr. Fleck promises to explain why being a geek is actually better than being a jock
jokes.punchline: His dramatized life graph of jock trajectories escalates through predictable failures (GPAs, scholarships) to arrive at alcoholism and used car sales — performed entirely through hand gestures and first-person voices
jokes.explanation: One of the better extended sequences in the episode. Mr. Fleck performing both sides of a jock's declining life with hand gestures is visually funny and character-rich. The escalation to 'bottle of booze, man' works. The performance element (inferred from the hand-acting description) makes this richer than it reads on the page. Good character integration — only Fleck would comfort kids this way.

jokes.text: 'What? I just blew my knee out during practice.' [pause] 'Oops, I'm out of school and selling used cars.'
jokes.setup: Fleck is acting out the jock's decline, building through athletic failure
jokes.punchline: The sudden time-jump from knee injury to selling used cars — with no intervening steps — captures how quickly the jock's world collapses; 'Oops' is a perfect word choice
jokes.explanation: The 'Oops' is doing a lot of heavy lifting — it's dismissive of an entire life derailment in a single syllable. The used car salesman as the nadir of jock ambitions is a well-worn trope but Fleck's delivery sells it. Economy is genuinely good here.

jokes.text: 'What? I'm head of a Fortune 500 company. And yes, Mr. Jock-Who-Cleaned-Me-Out, I will have fries with that.'
jokes.setup: Fleck pivots to narrating the geeks' superior future trajectories after cataloguing jock decline
jokes.punchline: The reversal fantasy — the bullied geek now the CEO ordering the former jock (now a fast food worker) to fetch him fries — is the classic nerd revenge fantasy articulated with total specificity including the direct address 'Mr. Jock-Who-Cleaned-Me-Out'
jokes.explanation: The callback to the clean-out ('Mr. Jock-Who-Cleaned-Me-Out') is a nice specific touch that elevates a familiar trope. The fantasy itself is not original but the phrasing is good and it lands as the emotional climax of Fleck's speech. Kids eat this up in context.

jokes.text: Neal: 'I thought this was supposed to make us feel better.' / Mr. Fleck: 'Hold on, Cool Breeze, it gets better.'
jokes.setup: Fleck's speech has so far catalogued geek suffering before promising a reversal
jokes.punchline: Neal honestly points out the speech isn't working yet; Fleck's 'Cool Breeze' is a bafflingly dated/hip nickname that undercuts his authority while asserting it
jokes.explanation: 'Cool Breeze' as a nickname is the kind of detail Freaks and Geeks does well — Fleck trying to connect with teens using slightly wrong vernacular. Neal's honest interruption is also very Neal: pragmatic, unimpressed, and direct.

jokes.text: Fleck produces a 16mm print of Monty Python and the Holy Grail as 'the simple pleasures of life'
jokes.setup: After his big speech about future success, Fleck promises to show them 'the simple pleasures of life'
jokes.punchline: The reveal is a print of Holy Grail — which, combined with the boys' rapturous reaction ('A.V. is paradise on earth'), is perfect because it's both genuinely nerdy-wonderful and completely validates the jocks' view of them
jokes.explanation: The scene earns its laughs through character authenticity. The elaborate trading chain (borrowed from A.V. guys at Lincoln who got it from an Air Force base, traded for 'Hemo the Magnificent') is absurdly specific and perfect. Bill's 'I'm so happy I think I'm gonna cry' is the capper. This is Freaks and Geeks at its best — comedy that's also genuinely sweet.

jokes.text: Fleck produces a 16mm print of Monty Python and the Holy Grail, explaining he traded 'Hemo the Magnificent' for it with the A.V. guys at Lincoln who got it from an Air Force base
jokes.setup: Fleck says the solution to teenage misery is 'the simple pleasures of life'
jokes.punchline: He produces a 16mm print of Monty Python via a convoluted chain (A.V. kids → Air Force base → traded for 'Hemo the Magnificent') — the bureaucratic absurdity of how he acquired it is as funny as the gift itself
jokes.explanation: The joke is layered — the Monty Python reveal is earned (geeks + Python is perfect), but the real joke is the ridiculous provenance chain and 'Hemo the Magnificent' as currency. Fleck's world is one where this is a totally normal transaction, which is funnier than the gift itself.

jokes.text: Bill: 'A.V. is paradise on earth. I'm so happy I think I'm gonna cry.'
jokes.setup: Fleck has just revealed the Holy Grail print
jokes.punchline: Bill's hyperbolic sincerity — 'paradise on earth' for an A.V. club film loan — delivered with total earnestness, no irony
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Bill Haverchuck. The joke works entirely because of who Bill is — a character so consistently sincere that his extreme emotional investment in a film loan is both funny and genuinely moving. The line 'I'm so happy I think I'm gonna cry' is delivered with complete seriousness and is one of the most Bill-specific moments in the episode. High character integration score justified.

jokes.text: Teacher: 'If you pass, you fly. If you fail, you get to look at my pretty face again next year.'
jokes.setup: Teacher announces it's test time with the standard 'pass or fail' stakes
jokes.punchline: The 'punishment' of failing is reframed as having to see the teacher again — his self-deprecating 'pretty face' comment is mild but humanizing
jokes.explanation: Serviceable teacher humor, gets a mild smile. The self-deprecation is fine but generic — any teacher in any show could say this line.

jokes.text: Dave Fleury broke his arm in gym: 'He fell off the ropes and missed the mat. It was hilarious.'
jokes.setup: The class's cheat sheet supplier is mysteriously absent; someone explains what happened
jokes.punchline: 'It was hilarious' tacked on to a description of someone's injury — casually cruel, delivered without guilt
jokes.explanation: The economy of 'It was hilarious' as a matter-of-fact addendum to an injury report is genuinely funny. The casual cruelty of high school — someone's misfortune reported with barely concealed delight — is observed accurately. The line also functions as a plot point, making it double-duty writing.

jokes.text: Principal on intercom: 'Better be a fire, bro.' — responding to the fire alarm Daniel pulled
jokes.setup: The fire alarm goes off (Daniel pulled it to avoid the math test)
jokes.punchline: 'Better be a fire, bro' — the principal's weary, almost threatening response, using 'bro' in a way that's somehow both trying to be relatable and deeply authoritative
jokes.explanation: Five words. 'Better be a fire, bro.' The 'bro' is the key — it's threatening and casual simultaneously, suggesting the principal has been through this too many times to even be properly angry. Economy is the strength here.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso to Daniel: 'You don't think you're the Fonz or something? If a jukebox was broken, think you could hit it and it'd start playing?'
jokes.setup: Mr. Rosso is confronting Daniel about pulling the fire alarm and being a cool-guy troublemaker
jokes.punchline: The Fonz comparison is so specific and slightly left-field as a disciplinary metaphor — Rosso is a guidance counselor who uses pop culture analogies that are just a bit off
jokes.explanation: Rosso's habit of slightly-off pop culture references as counseling tools is a great recurring character trait. The Fonz comparison is both an insult AND a compliment, which Daniel correctly recognizes. 'If a jukebox was broken, think you could hit it and it'd start playing?' extends the metaphor one step further than necessary in a way that's very Rosso.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso: 'Do you like them apples?' — after delivering a threat
jokes.setup: Rosso finishes his lecture to Daniel about taking advantage of his niceness
jokes.punchline: 'Do you like them apples?' — a tough-guy signing-off phrase that Rosso deploys with complete seriousness, which is slightly more aggressive and also slightly more dorky than the situation warrants
jokes.explanation: Another Rosso character note — he thinks he's much cooler and more intimidating than he is. The phrase works because it's deployed in a school counselor context.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso's 'punishment' for Daniel: 'You're now in A.V.'
jokes.setup: Rosso has dramatically built to revealing Daniel's punishment
jokes.punchline: Being assigned to A.V. — the dorkiest place in school — as disciplinary action, framed by Rosso as 'a real cool way for you to spend the rest of the school year'
jokes.explanation: The joke functions on two levels: it's humiliating for Daniel, and it becomes ironically the catalyst for his arc toward genuine connection. Rosso calling it 'a real cool way' with complete sincerity is the character touch that elevates it.

jokes.text: Lindsay to teacher: 'You're quitting?' — when teacher announces someone will be 'very very happy'
jokes.setup: Ms. Kelly announces she has news that will make someone 'very, very happy'
jokes.punchline: Lindsay immediately asks 'You're quitting?' — revealing her relationship with school is now so adversarial she assumes the best possible news would be losing a teacher
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best jokes in the episode. It's perfectly calibrated to Lindsay's season-long arc — her drift away from being a good student into cynicism about school — and delivered as an instinctive blurt rather than a calculated insult, which makes it funnier and more damning. The class titters at it, Ms. Kelly has to manage it, and it tells us everything about where Lindsay is psychologically. High character integration fully earned.

jokes.text: Lindsay, responding to being told she should be thrilled about the Academic Summit: 'Um... I'm one lucky girl?'
jokes.setup: Ms. Kelly has waited for Lindsay's enthusiastic response to the Academic Summit news
jokes.punchline: Lindsay recycles the teacher's own phrase back at her with zero enthusiasm and a questioning inflection — technically compliant, completely hollow
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's deflated parroting of the teacher's words is sharp. The question mark inflection transforms a factual statement into a surrender. It's economical, character-specific, and tells us a lot about where Lindsay is in her arc. The callback to the teacher's own phrase is an elegant economy move.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'How can I be in the top 1%? I don't study that much. I mean, are the kids in Michigan schools that stupid?'
jokes.setup: Ms. Kelly has revealed Lindsay is academically exceptional
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's response is to question the competence of Michigan's student population rather than accept she might actually be smart — a defensive intellectualism that's both funny and revealing
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's refusal to accept a compliment about her intelligence by insulting the competition is a very specific character moment. The insult-as-humility is funny and also slightly obnoxious in a realistic teenager way.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso: 'When I was in college, back in the early 1700s...'
jokes.setup: Rosso is trying to relate to Lindsay by sharing a personal college memory
jokes.punchline: He pre-emptively dates himself with 'back in the early 1700s' — self-deprecating about his age in a way that's so exaggerated it lands as a mild absurdism
jokes.explanation: A mild, pleasant joke that gets a mild, pleasant reaction. The 'early 1700s' as an exaggeration is a common bit but delivered with Rosso's particular earnestness. Functional but not special.

jokes.text: A.V. club member: 'I got kinda woozy when I had to show that girls' time-of-the-month film. They didn't even let me into the class to show it.' / Harris: 'I heard that's because you could see a girl's boobs in it.'
jokes.setup: A.V. club members are discussing their film-showing duties and the one film they weren't allowed to present
jokes.punchline: Harris's 'I heard that's because you could see a girl's boobs in it' as the presumed reason for their exclusion from the health class — delivered with complete authority and zero self-awareness
jokes.explanation: Harris's confident transmission of completely unverified information as fact is a character note that works well. The content (teenage boys' obsession with any female nudity, even educational) is accurate and the delivery is deadpan. The A.V. member's wooziness over a menstruation film also lands as honest awkward-teen comedy.

jokes.text: Daniel enters the A.V. room and is greeted by a nerd who cheerfully asks: 'Hey, Daniel, do you know how to fix a projector?' / Daniel: 'No.' / 'We can teach ya!' / 'Great.'
jokes.setup: Daniel, the coolest kid in school, has just been sentenced to A.V. as punishment
jokes.punchline: His flat 'Great.' in response to being offered projector repair lessons — maximum controlled contempt in minimum words, followed by resigned acceptance
jokes.explanation: The 'Great.' functions as the perfect encapsulation of Daniel's situation — delivered flatly, it could mean anything but clearly means the opposite of great. The enthusiastic geek and the defeated cool kid share one of the episode's better comic contrasts.

jokes.text: The geeks discuss Daniel: 'I bet that Daniel guy is high on drugs, and if we make him angry, he'll freak out and wreck the A.V. room.'
jokes.setup: Sam, Neal, and Bill are panicking about Daniel invading their A.V. sanctum
jokes.punchline: Bill's specific fear — that Daniel is currently high AND will specifically wreck the A.V. room if angered — is both an exaggeration and a completely plausible worst-case scenario from a nervous nerd's perspective
jokes.explanation: Bill's catastrophizing is character-true and the specificity of 'wreck the A.V. room' (his specific sacred space) makes it funnier than a generic 'he'll hurt us.' The fear is sincere and slightly disproportionate in the right way.

jokes.text: Neal: 'Sam, he gave you a porno. I wouldn't exactly say you have a meaningful relationship with him.'
jokes.setup: Sam argues they shouldn't assume the worst about Daniel because 'he's friends with my sister'
jokes.punchline: Neal's flat correction — the only interaction Sam can claim with Daniel is a pornographic film being handed to him — as a counter-argument to 'meaningful relationship'
jokes.explanation: One of the sharpest lines in the episode. Neal's logic is impeccable and cutting — 'he gave you a porno' as the full extent of their relationship, stated as a simple fact to deflate Sam's generous interpretation. The economy is excellent and it's a callback to earlier season events that rewards viewers. Very Neal: correct, direct, and slightly superior.

jokes.text: Teacher: 'Welcome to the 1% club. I was one back in 1956. Look what it did for me!'
jokes.setup: A teacher congratulates Lindsay on being selected for the Academic Summit
jokes.punchline: 'Look what it did for me!' — said by a person who is still a teacher, implying the academic achievement led to... a modest teaching career. Self-deprecating about his own life without realizing he's undermining the point
jokes.explanation: Nicely dark. The teacher is trying to be encouraging but accidentally demonstrates that academic excellence didn't get him very far either. Plays into the episode's theme of questioning whether conventional achievement paths are worth it.

jokes.text: Deadhead girl: 'I wish I'd never heard it, just so I could hear it again for the first time.'
jokes.setup: A Grateful Dead fan is passionately recommending American Beauty to Lindsay
jokes.punchline: The classic Deadhead hyperbole — wishing she could un-hear something to re-experience it — is so earnest and recognizable as a certain kind of music fan that it's more cringe-affectionate than mocking
jokes.explanation: Mild type-comedy about a very specific kind of music fan. Works because it's recognizable and delivered with total sincerity. Not especially original but accurately observed.

jokes.text: Sara on teaching Ken to dance: 'You'll probably learn even faster than that, because you'll have both of us teaching you at the same time.'
jokes.setup: Sara is trying to recruit Ken into disco lessons, pointing out she taught Nick in less than an hour
jokes.punchline: The cheerful logic — two teachers must produce twice the learning speed — is presented as encouragement but is actually a mild insult (Nick required an hour, Ken should require less) and completely ignores Ken's personality
jokes.explanation: Mild. The comedy is mostly in Ken's reaction (his 'Um... No?' is the actual joke) rather than Sara's line itself.

jokes.text: Ken's response to the disco lesson invitation: 'Um... No?'
jokes.setup: Sara has cheerfully insisted Ken will learn to disco dance even faster than Nick did
jokes.punchline: Ken's 'Um... No?' — the questioning inflection suggesting he's slightly baffled that this even needs to be said, the pause suggesting he's processing whether there's a polite way to say this
jokes.explanation: Two words (one of them is 'um') and it's one of Ken's best moments. The question mark on 'No?' is doing all the work — Ken isn't certain the question was serious enough to require a serious answer. Maximum character with minimum words. Economy score reflects this.

jokes.text: Ken: 'All right. I give up, Nick. When does Alan Funt come boogieing out?'
jokes.setup: Ken is watching Nick dance with Sara and cannot process that this is real
jokes.punchline: The Candid Camera reference — Ken's theory is that Nick's disco conversion must be an elaborate prank — perfectly captures his disbelief. 'Come boogieing out' adds the perfect specific touch.
jokes.explanation: The Alan Funt/Candid Camera reference is perfectly period-appropriate (1980) and specific in a way that 'is this a joke?' wouldn't be. 'Come boogieing out' adds absurdism — the mental image of Alan Funt boogieing is involuntarily funny. Strong Ken voice: his disbelief expressed through a highly specific cultural reference.

jokes.text: Ken on Nick's disco conversion: 'Do I need to remind you that you used to hate disco?'
jokes.setup: Nick is defending Sara and his new disco lifestyle
jokes.punchline: Ken framing Nick's music identity change as something Nick might have literally forgotten — as if it needs to be brought to his attention — rather than a choice
jokes.explanation: Ken's mock-patient reminder that Nick 'used to hate disco' is a solid character line — treating an identity shift as if it might be amnesia. Functional and character-true.

jokes.text: Nick's defense of disco: 'What's the difference between Zeppelin and disco, really? No, listen, they both have heavy drums and they have bass. Have you heard that Foxy song, Hot Number? It rocks, man.'
jokes.setup: Ken has challenged Nick's disco conversion
jokes.punchline: Nick's sincere musicological defense of disco — equating it with Led Zeppelin through the 'drums and bass' argument — is both funny as an argument and charming as a character moment. The specific citation of 'Hot Number' as evidence is perfect.
jokes.explanation: This is Nick Andopolis in miniature — a person of deep enthusiasms that shift completely based on who he's dating, but who brings genuine passion to each new thing. The Zeppelin/disco equivalence argument is both wrong and weirdly coherent. High character integration score justified.

jokes.text: Ken: 'No! I don't know that song, thank God. But I can guarantee it sucks.'
jokes.setup: Nick has cited a specific Foxy disco song as evidence disco rocks
jokes.punchline: Ken's confident pre-judgment — he hasn't heard it, 'thank God,' but is certain it sucks — is the logic of ideological musical identity rather than actual taste. 'Thank God' as relief at ignorance is the key touch.
jokes.explanation: Ken's 'thank God' for his ignorance is the specificity that makes this more than just 'disco sucks' bravado. He's relieved he doesn't know it because knowing it might compromise his certainty. Accurate teen music tribalism.

jokes.text: Ken to Lindsay: 'Lindsay, can you please start going out with Nick again? I can't take much more of this.'
jokes.setup: Ken is suffering through Nick's disco phase, which he attributes to Nick trying to make Lindsay jealous
jokes.punchline: Ken's solution — asking his friend's ex-girlfriend to resume dating him to end the disco menace — is completely self-centered and barely disguised as concern for Nick
jokes.explanation: Very funny for its naked self-interest and the flatness with which Ken delivers it. He's not pretending this is for Nick's benefit — it's entirely about his own suffering through disco. The 'I can't take much more of this' is perfectly phrased as personal complaint. Strong Ken voice.

jokes.text: Harold: 'Our daughter in the top 1%. Like my grandpa used to say, if I was wearing my vest, all the buttons would pop.'
jokes.setup: Harold is expressing pride about Lindsay's Academic Summit selection
jokes.punchline: The vest/buttons-popping idiom is specific to Harold — an old-fashioned, slightly corny expression of pride that is somehow both charming and faintly embarrassing
jokes.explanation: The idiom itself is the joke — it's so Harold, so of a particular Midwestern dad generation, delivered completely sincerely. The phrase is attributed to 'my grandpa' which makes it even more archaic. High character integration score is fully earned — this could only be Harold.

jokes.text: Harold: 'Wacky? Can it! You're going to that summit, Lindsay. This isn't even open for debate.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has been tentatively expressing doubt about the summit ('It sounds kinda... dumb')
jokes.punchline: 'Can it!' — Harold's slightly old-fashioned authoritative command, combined with shutting down a 'debate' before it started. The escalation from 'just sounds dumb' to Harold going full dad-command mode is disproportionate in a funny way.
jokes.explanation: 'Can it!' is such a specific Harold phrase — dated, slightly silly, but delivered with full paternal authority. The joke is partly that Harold's anger is disproportionate to Lindsay's mild dissatisfaction, and partly the archaic vocabulary.

jokes.text: A.V. teacher announces 'movie day' — it's Franco Zeffirelli's Romeo and Juliet. If A.V. member 'would get his butt in here'...
jokes.setup: The teacher cheerfully sets up movie day as a reward for surviving finals
jokes.punchline: The movie is Romeo and Juliet (already comedically undermined as an earnest classroom text); the 'A.V. member' the teacher is waiting on turns out to be Daniel Desario — the coolest kid in school now reduced to projector duty
jokes.explanation: The humor is situational — Daniel, who pulled a fire alarm to escape a test, now has to show up to run a projector for Romeo and Juliet. The contrast between his self-image and his current duties is the joke.

jokes.text: A.V. Teacher to Daniel: 'I was starting to think I'd have to act out the film myself.'
jokes.setup: Daniel arrives late to run the projector
jokes.punchline: The teacher's dry comment about acting out Romeo and Juliet himself — said with light menace/resignation — is a nice character beat. The mental image of the A.V. teacher doing Shakespeare is mildly funny.
jokes.explanation: Mild. The joke is in the image but the teacher isn't established well enough as a character for this to land as a strong character beat. Functional filler.

jokes.text: A student fake-coughs 'GEEK!' at Daniel while he's setting up the projector in a rival class
jokes.setup: Daniel, the coolest guy in school, is now doing A.V. work in front of a classroom of students
jokes.punchline: Being called a geek — the exact identity he's spent his entire high school career avoiding — through the cowardly fake-cough method, which is itself a classic geek-baiting technique
jokes.explanation: The reversal is sharp — Daniel, who has probably been on the other end of this kind of mockery his whole life, is now receiving it for the same crime he'd have laughed at others for. The fake-cough delivery is properly humiliating. Good structural irony.

jokes.text: The projector crashes to the floor. The teacher asks if Daniel needs help. Daniel: 'No, I think I got it.' Then continues struggling.
jokes.setup: Daniel is fumbling with unfamiliar A.V. equipment in front of a classroom
jokes.punchline: The too-proud 'No, I think I got it' after crashing the equipment — classic male pride refusing assistance — followed by continued incompetence
jokes.explanation: Standard fish-out-of-water slapstick. Works in context because of who Daniel is, but the joke itself isn't original. The visual comedy of the cool kid fumbling with A.V. equipment is more compelling than any specific line.

jokes.text: Teacher: 'Any chance of being ready in the near future?' / Daniel: 'Yeah, just one second, all right?' / Teacher: 'OK. Ready.' / Daniel: 'Ready.' / Teacher: 'Saints be praised.'
jokes.setup: The class has been waiting forever for Daniel to get the projector working
jokes.punchline: 'Saints be praised' — the teacher's theatrical relief when Daniel finally manages it is both funny and slightly condescending, as if Daniel accomplished something miraculous rather than basic
jokes.explanation: 'Saints be praised' is a good phrase in a slightly theatrical way. The extended projector-setup sequence earns a decent laugh as cringe comedy — Daniel's humiliation is sustained and specific.

jokes.text: Daniel hits the malfunctioning projector. A.V. Teacher: 'Instead of hitting it, could you please just make the film loop a little bigger?'
jokes.setup: The projector is making noise; Daniel defaults to the Fonzie move of hitting it
jokes.punchline: The teacher's correction — not 'don't hit it' but the specific technical alternative — and the callback to the earlier Fonzie conversation (if Daniel thinks he can hit machines to fix them)
jokes.explanation: This is a beautiful callback — Rosso compared Daniel to the Fonz (who fixes jukeboxes by hitting them), and now Daniel actually IS hitting a machine to fix it, exactly like the Fonz. The teacher's patient technical correction ('make the film loop a little bigger') shows Daniel is in completely foreign territory. The callback elevates what would otherwise be a simple slapstick moment.

jokes.text: Daniel: 'Man, I can't believe I have to go to A.V. again today. It's so stupid.' / Kim: 'Well, you know, Daniel, nobody asked you to pull the fire alarm.' / Daniel: 'Oh, what was I supposed to do, flunk the test? I suck at math. I suck at everything.'
jokes.setup: Daniel complaining about his A.V. punishment to Kim
jokes.punchline: His justification escalates from 'I suck at math' to the bleak 'I suck at everything' — stated without self-pity, more as a simple reported fact, which makes it both funny and unexpectedly sad
jokes.explanation: The pivot from math-specific failure to general failure is the key move — 'I suck at everything' said without drama, as a matter of fact. This is Freaks and Geeks' signature blend: the line is funny and also quietly devastating. Daniel's self-awareness about his own limitations, delivered without complaint, is strong character writing.

jokes.text: Kim to Daniel: 'Why don't you go tell it to the fire alarm?'
jokes.setup: Kim has been listening to Daniel complain about his problems while noting he never listens to hers
jokes.punchline: Kim's 'fire alarm' comeback — a callback to the very action that started his current problems, suggesting he should confide in the inanimate object since the fire alarm apparently gets his attention
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. Kim's 'go tell it to the fire alarm' is sharp, economical, and character-specific — it's a callback to Daniel's earlier action that recontextualizes it as self-reliance/avoidance. The line works as a devastating put-down AND as a funny callback. High economy score justified: six words that do a lot. This is Kim at her sharpest.

jokes.text: Bill lobbying for his new D&D character name: 'I'm Gorthon the Thief. Surrender your jewels, Princess!'
jokes.setup: The geeks are discussing new D&D characters
jokes.punchline: Bill's immediate performance in character — 'Surrender your jewels, Princess!' — delivered with probably too much commitment, demonstrating that for Bill this is completely real
jokes.explanation: The joke is Bill's full-commitment instant character immersion — 'Surrender your jewels' as a natural mode of speech that he switches into without transition. Pure Bill: no irony, total investment.

jokes.text: Neal: 'Oh, Bill, don't be a thief again! You always fall down a well trying to steal stuff.'
jokes.setup: Bill has announced his new character will be a thief
jokes.punchline: Neal's complaint reveals that Bill has tried the thief character before with consistent catastrophic results — falling down wells during theft attempts is apparently a recurring pattern specific to Bill's D&D play style
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'you always fall down a well trying to steal stuff' is one of the episode's best details. It implies a rich history of Bill's catastrophic D&D thievery involving wells specifically — this is exactly the kind of specificity that makes Freaks and Geeks' character comedy work. You can see all the previous sessions where this happened.

jokes.text: Daniel's invitation to D&D: 'I couldn't do that.' / Neal immediately: 'Yeah, you wouldn't like it, anyway. It's really boring.'
jokes.setup: Sam has invited Daniel to D&D night; Daniel hesitates
jokes.punchline: Neal's immediate backpedaling — the second Daniel shows any reluctance, Neal withdraws the invitation and declares it 'really boring' — the insecurity of the geek who doesn't want to be rejected showing up as preemptive self-deprecation
jokes.explanation: Neal's instant self-protective withdrawal is character comedy at its best. He loves D&D passionately (see: 'Kragenmore the Destroyer') but the second a cool kid might reject it, he's the first to call it boring. This is painfully accurate teenage behavior and specific to Neal's character mix of superiority complex and deep insecurity.

jokes.text: Harris describing the Deities & Demigods handbook: 'Have you guys seen the drawing of the goddess Ishtar? Hubba hubba! You should look at the goddess of Vice. I just hope you can see her butt.'
jokes.setup: Harris is hyping the new D&D handbook to the geeks
jokes.punchline: Harris's sexual enthusiasm for mythological drawings in a D&D handbook is simultaneously very teenager and very specific to his particular brand of geek passion
jokes.explanation: 'I just hope you can see her butt' about a D&D deity illustration is pure Harris — his hormones filtered through arcane fantasy references. 'Hubba hubba!' is also a perfect period-specific Harris detail.

jokes.text: Sam selling D&D to Daniel: 'You get to pretend to be somebody you can't be in real life.'
jokes.setup: Sam is trying to convince Daniel to join D&D night
jokes.punchline: Sam means this as straightforwardly appealing, but for Daniel it lands as an unintentionally precise description of his situation — he literally can't be the person he wants to be in real life (not academically struggling, not marginalized to A.V.)
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most layered lines. It's simultaneously Sam's earnest pitch, an accidentally accurate description of Daniel's predicament, and the thematic statement of the whole D&D subplot. The line works on multiple levels simultaneously — comedic, emotional, and thematic. This is Freaks and Geeks at its best: jokes that carry real weight.

jokes.text: Neal to Daniel: 'You can't possibly be any worse than Bill.' / Bill: 'Hey!'
jokes.setup: Daniel has agreed to try D&D but disclaims 'Don't expect me to be good at it'
jokes.punchline: Neal's 'You can't possibly be any worse than Bill' is both an encouragement and an insult — using Bill as the floor of competence — with Bill's offended 'Hey!' as the perfect capper
jokes.explanation: A setup/punchline/reaction triple that works because the callback (Bill always falls down wells) justifies Neal's assessment. Bill's 'Hey!' isn't truly offended — he knows it's true. The economy is excellent: twelve words, three character notes.

jokes.text: Harris, after Sam decides to play: 'Thought you were too cool to play... Logan the Huge.'
jokes.setup: Sam had been acting too cool for D&D; now he's capitulated
jokes.punchline: Harris naming Sam's character 'Logan the Huge' while calling him out for his pretension — the name is both grandiose and hilarious given Sam's actual stature, and delivers the mockery inside the game's own language
jokes.explanation: Harris delivers the cut perfectly — 'Logan the Huge' does double duty as Sam's D&D character name and as an ironic comment on Sam's momentary pretension. The name itself is funny (implying Sam's normal persona is anything but huge). Good economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Disco DJ to Ken: 'Oh, check it out, kids. It's our little opinionated rock 'n' roller friend.' / Ken: 'Who the hell's he talkin' about?' / Lindsay: 'I think he's talking about you.'
jokes.setup: Ken has been causing trouble in the disco venue
jokes.punchline: Ken's genuine confusion about being described as an 'opinionated rock 'n' roller friend' — which is exactly what he is — followed by Lindsay's flat 'I think he's talking about you'
jokes.explanation: Ken's inability to recognize himself in an accurate description is good character comedy — he's so inside his own identity that hearing it described from outside is disorienting. Lindsay's flat confirmation is the right capper.

jokes.text: Harris explaining the Dungeon Master role: 'I don't have a character. I'm the Dungeon Master. I control the game and act out all the characters you'll meet along the way.' / Daniel: 'What, you're some super-smart guy?' / Harris: 'I don't have a character.'
jokes.setup: Daniel is trying to understand D&D hierarchy
jokes.punchline: Daniel's incredulous 'What, you're some super-smart guy?' as his interpretation of Dungeon Master — and Harris's patient, literal repeat of his explanation as if Daniel must have misheard
jokes.explanation: Harris's patient repeat of his explanation without engaging with Daniel's 'super-smart' interpretation is good deadpan — he's not going to dignify the mischaracterization, but he's also not going to fight it.

jokes.text: Bill on Harris being Dungeon Master: 'He thinks being a Dungeon Master gives him a license to mess with our heads.' / Harris: 'Oh, I'm sorry. Perhaps I should let you encounter kittens and grandmas so as not to upset you.'
jokes.setup: Bill and Neal are warning Daniel about Harris's dungeon mastering style
jokes.punchline: Harris's sarcastic counter-offer of 'kittens and grandmas' as alternatives to his actual dungeon challenges — delivered with perfect contempt
jokes.explanation: Harris's 'kittens and grandmas' line is the best Harris moment in the episode and arguably the episode's best pure joke. The specificity of the pairing is what makes it — not just 'easy enemies' but the specific image of grandmas as gentle D&D opposition. The contempt is perfectly calibrated. High economy: the phrase itself generates a vivid absurd image with seven words. Very Harris — disdainful, superior, but within his specific domain of expertise.

jokes.text: Ken confronting the disco DJ: 'This place is empty, man! Disco is dead. Give it up!' / DJ response: 'Why don't you go back to sellin' shirts at the mall, buddy?'
jokes.setup: Ken has interrupted the disco to make a statement about the death of disco
jokes.punchline: The DJ's 'sellin' shirts at the mall' insult — implying Ken's rock identity is retail — is a very specific class-based burn that catches Ken off guard
jokes.explanation: The 'sellin' shirts at the mall' insult is pleasantly specific — it's the period-accurate version of a rocker being accused of selling out to retail. The Ken/DJ confrontation overall is funny but this line is the best of the exchange.

jokes.text: DJ sings 'I Will Survive' to a nearly empty disco to weak applause after Ken's outburst
jokes.setup: The DJ has been defending disco's vitality against Ken's 'disco is dead' proclamation
jokes.punchline: He demonstrates disco's survival by performing 'I Will Survive' to an almost empty venue — the song's defiance made pathetic by the context, the 'weak applause' noted
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual/situational irony — 'I Will Survive' as a disco anthem of defiance, performed by a dying disco DJ in a half-empty venue, with 'weak applause.' The song choice is inspired: it perfectly captures the tragicomedy of the moment. Ken's proclamation that 'Disco is dead' is immediately followed by the music's own desperate denial. Strong structure.

jokes.text: Daniel rolling his dice, getting assigned Dwarf, then naming himself 'Carlos.' Neal: 'Carlos the Dwarf?' / Daniel: 'Yeah, you got a problem with that, Gorthon?'
jokes.setup: Daniel is unhappy being assigned a dwarf character and wants something cool
jokes.punchline: He names his dwarf 'Carlos' — a completely non-fantasy, ethnically specific, extremely non-D&D name — then defends it aggressively when questioned, addressing Bill by his character name ('Gorthon') without irony
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode, easily. 'Carlos the Dwarf' is funny on multiple levels: it's a perfectly non-fantasy name chosen by someone who can't quite commit to fantasy naming conventions; it's Daniel asserting his identity even within a game he barely understands; his defense of it ('Yeah, you got a problem with that, Gorthon?') shows he's already adopted D&D language while remaining completely himself; and addressing Bill as 'Gorthon' shows unconscious absorption into the game world. It's quotable, highly original within the context, completely character-specific, and beautifully economical. 'Carlos the Dwarf' as a phrase is genuinely funny independent of context. This is what 8+ looks like.

jokes.text: Nick to Ken after being embarrassed at the disco: 'Give me a call when you get over your Saturday Night Fever. And you're too tall to be a good dancer!'
jokes.setup: Ken has just gotten Nick thrown out of his own dance contest by starting a fight with the DJ
jokes.punchline: Nick's parting shots: 'Saturday Night Fever' as an insult directed at Ken (who hates disco, making it a perfectly wrong label), and 'You're too tall to be a good dancer' — a very specific grievance that may or may not be a real issue
jokes.explanation: The 'Saturday Night Fever' insult directed at Ken (the anti-disco guy) is nicely backwards. 'You're too tall to be a good dancer' is the funnier line — specific, seemingly technical, and slightly absurd as a parting shot.

jokes.text: DJ cheerfully to Ken: 'Hey, I agree with you, man. That place sucks. And you're right. They're closing the disco next week and bringing in foxy boxing. You should come back and check it out.' / Ken [softly]: 'Rock 'n' roll!'
jokes.setup: Ken has just been confrontational with the DJ about disco being dead
jokes.punchline: The DJ pivots entirely, agreeing with Ken and announcing the disco's closure — validating Ken's whole argument — but replacing it with 'foxy boxing.' Ken's delighted 'rock 'n' roll!' under his breath is the capper
jokes.explanation: A satisfying payoff to Ken's anti-disco crusade — but the victory is hollow since 'foxy boxing' as the replacement is both a period-accurate absurdism and not exactly a cultural upgrade. Ken's small, private 'Rock 'n' roll!' is perfect: he's won but the win means almost nothing. Strong structure and good character landing.

jokes.text: Nick, explaining his self-improvement to Lindsay: 'I even quit smoking pot. Yeah. I don't even really want it anymore. It's like I totally cleaned myself up.'
jokes.setup: Nick is earnestly telling Lindsay all the positive changes he's made with Sara
jokes.punchline: The irony that Nick's 'cleaning himself up' has produced a disco-obsessed, smoke-free young man who is somehow more embarrassing than his former stoned-drummer self — the improvement is real but the result is funny
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is largely situational — Nick's earnest pride in his improvement while wearing a disco outfit is funnier than any specific line. The self-improvement narrative in this context is gently ironic.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'You seem like you're having way more fun with her than you ever did with me.' / Nick (pause): 'Yeah. I mean, I am.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has been gracious about Nick's new girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Nick's honest confirmation — after a beat — that yes, he is having more fun. The pause suggests he briefly considers being polite then decides against it. The brutal honesty is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Freaks and Geeks' signature tonal blend: it's funny because Nick's honesty is so unguarded, and sad because Lindsay clearly expected a denial. The pause before 'Yeah. I mean, I am.' is where the comedy and the pathos live simultaneously. Nick isn't being cruel — he's just genuinely incapable of managing his truthfulness. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: The next disco contestant is announced: 'Eugene.' Then Eugene turns out to be magically good, prompting Sara: 'What the heck? They didn't say you were allowed to do magic!'
jokes.setup: Nick has just finished a strong performance; Sara is confident he'll win
jokes.punchline: The reveal of Eugene as a ringer — who apparently incorporates actual magic tricks into his disco routine — with Sara's completely earnest complaint about magic not being disclosed in the rules
jokes.explanation: Sara's 'They didn't say you were allowed to do magic!' is one of the episode's best lines. It's funny for multiple reasons: the implication that magic is a recognized (if prohibited) category in disco competitions; Sara's complete seriousness about an absurd complaint; and the specificity of 'they didn't say' (she's invoking an unstated rule). The name 'Eugene' as the competition winner is also a comedy choice — the most anti-disco possible name for a disco champion. High originality: nobody sees the 'disco magic' twist coming.

jokes.text: Response to Eugene's magic-disco act: 'Wow. He's really good.' — delivered with genuine wonder
jokes.setup: Nick's camp was expecting him to easily win; Eugene has appeared and is combining disco with magic tricks
jokes.punchline: Someone in Nick's corner simply conceding 'Wow. He's really good.' — the understated acknowledgment that Eugene is a genuinely superior competitor is funnier than any protest
jokes.explanation: The straight reaction to Eugene is funnier than any joke could be. The deadpan concession 'Wow. He's really good.' — in a moment where loyalty should prevent such acknowledgment — is perfectly timed and executed. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: Daniel at the end of D&D: 'Greetings, Princess. It is I, Carlos the Dwarf. The dragon has been slain, and you're free to rule your kingdom.'
jokes.setup: Daniel has completed his first D&D campaign as Carlos the Dwarf
jokes.punchline: Daniel's in-character speech is delivered with complete commitment — 'It is I, Carlos the Dwarf' with the grammatically formal 'It is I' — fully absorbed into the fiction. The name callback is the capper.
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'Carlos the Dwarf' pays off beautifully. Daniel's complete commitment to the character — using formal in-game speech, 'It is I, Carlos the Dwarf' — shows total absorption into D&D within a single session. This is Daniel finding something genuine. The comedy is in the contrast between his cool-kid persona and the complete sincerity with which he announces his dwarf character's victory. High character integration because this moment only works because of who Daniel is.

jokes.text: Neal and Sam after Daniel leaves: 'Does him wanting to play with us again mean that he's turning into a geek... or we're turning into cool guys?' / Sam: 'I don't know. But I'm gonna go for us being cool guys.'
jokes.setup: Daniel has enthusiastically asked to play again, which is unexpected
jokes.punchline: The boys' immediate reframing of the situation — not that they've recruited a geek but that they've been retroactively promoted to cool — and Sam's decisive 'gonna go for us being cool guys'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most thematically resonant jokes, and it lands as both comedy and a quietly moving character moment. Neal's question articulates the episode's central ambiguity with perfect clarity, and Sam's 'I'm gonna go for us being cool guys' is both wishful thinking and a kind of earned confidence. The laugh comes from the instant reinterpretation of events to favor their self-image, but the moment also genuinely works as a coming-of-age beat. Strong character integration for both boys.

jokes.text: The geeks debate whether Daniel wanting to play D&D again means he's 'turning into a geek' or they're 'turning into cool guys' — then unanimously agree they're definitely cool guys
jokes.setup: Daniel has just expressed genuine enthusiasm for D&D and asked to play again
jokes.punchline: The geeks instantly reframe the situation as evidence of their own coolness rather than Daniel's geekification — and agree unanimously on the more flattering interpretation
jokes.explanation: This exchange is the episode's thematic conclusion expressed as a perfect comedy beat. The geeks' immediate, unanimous preference for the self-flattering interpretation ('we're cool guys') is both funny and quietly moving — it's a small act of communal self-esteem. The structure (one poses the dilemma, the other immediately picks the better option, all agree) is executed with sitcom precision. Very high character integration; only these specific characters in this moment can deliver this beat.

jokes.text: Neal to Lindsay at departure: 'I got something for you. It's chocolates for a sweet trip. Yeah, we always give my grandma chocolates when we put her on the bus. That, and we pin her name and address on her jacket in case she gets lost.'
jokes.setup: Neal has rushed up to give Lindsay a send-off gift
jokes.punchline: The reveal that his send-off tradition comes from putting elderly grandmothers on buses, complete with the detail about pinning her name and address to her jacket — comparing Lindsay to a confused elderly person who might get lost
jokes.explanation: Neal's chocolate story is one of the episode's best jokes. The structure is excellent: 'chocolates for a sweet trip' sounds sweet, then the grandma-on-the-bus origin story undercuts it, then the 'name and address on her jacket' escalation makes it properly dark. Neal doesn't realize he's insulting Lindsay by comparison. The total obliviousness to how this sounds is perfectly Neal. Economy is good — the joke earns its length through the escalation.

jokes.text: Neal adds: 'That, and we pin her name and address on her jacket in case she gets lost.'
jokes.setup: Neal has just compared Lindsay's departure to putting grandma on a bus
jokes.punchline: The escalation detail — the name tag pinned to grandma's jacket — pushes the comparison further and implies grandma's cognitive situation is more precarious than grandma-bus-protocol alone would suggest
jokes.explanation: The escalation from chocolates to name-pinned-on-jacket is excellent two-beat construction. The first reveal (grandma bus) is good; the second reveal (grandma might get lost) is better. The image of an adult having their name and address pinned to their jacket like a lost child is specific and darkly funny. Neal delivers it as completely normal, which is the whole joke. Strong escalation structure and high quotability.

jokes.text: Bill didn't bring a gift. Neal: 'Hey, he didn't get you anything!' / Bill: 'Oh. That's just for coming down. It didn't cost me a cent.'
jokes.setup: Neal has given Lindsay chocolates; Bill has given nothing
jokes.punchline: Bill's completely unashamed response — his presence is the gift, literally, and he's happy to clarify it was free — delivered with total transparency and no embarrassment
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Bill. 'It didn't cost me a cent' — he's noting his own cheapness as if it's a feature rather than a bug. The unself-conscious transparency of this, delivered without defensiveness, is completely Bill Haverchuck. High character integration score fully justified: this is one of the most Bill-specific moments in the series. Neal's scandalized tattling ('Hey, he didn't get you anything!') sets it up perfectly.

tmdb_overview: Neal wrestles with the dilemma of whether to tell his mother about his father's affair. Lindsay is given detention after coming to the aid of a girl being attacked by a boy. Daniel deals with pressures at home and with Kim. He escapes from his problems through punk music.
